g.data-narration: Welcome to learning Java Interfaces - a fundamental concept for achieving abstraction and polymorphism in Java.
An interface is a reference type that defines a contract - a set of method signatures that implementing classes must provide.
g.text: A reference type that defines a
contract of methods that implementing classes must provide.
Used for full abstraction and defining blueprints.

g.data-narration: Interfaces enable multiple inheritance in Java, promote loose coupling, and define contracts without implementation details.
Interfaces declare methods that are implicitly public and abstract. No implementation is provided in the interface declaration.
A class implements an interface using the 'implements' keyword and provides concrete implementations for all declared methods.
Java allows a class to implement multiple interfaces, enabling a form of multiple inheritance without the diamond problem.
All variables in an interface are implicitly public static final - they are constants that belong to the interface itself.
Java 8 introduced default methods, allowing interfaces to provide implementations. Classes can override or inherit them.
g.text: default int add(int a, int b) {
Enables backward compatibility - add new methods
to interfaces without breaking existing implementations.

g.data-narration: Java 8 also allows static methods in interfaces. These belong to the interface itself, not to implementing classes.
g.text: static int multiply(int a, int b) {
Static methods belong to interface, not implementing class.

g.data-narration: Java 9 introduced private methods in interfaces, enabling code reuse within the interface while hiding implementation details.
g.text: default int add(int a, int b) {
private int addInternal(int a, int b) {
implementation details and reduce code duplication.

g.data-narration: Interfaces and abstract classes serve different purposes. Understanding their differences is crucial for proper Java design.
g.text: Interface vs Abstract Class - Key Differences

g.data-narration: This comparison shows how interfaces support multiple inheritance while abstract classes cannot, along with other structural differences.
Interfaces are extensively used in Java APIs. Java Collections uses List, Set, Map for different data structures.
g.text: Interfaces define contracts enabling polymorphism and flexible, loosely-coupled code designs.

g.data-narration: Key points: interfaces declare with 'interface', implement with 'implements'. Methods are public abstract, variables are constants.
g.text: Declared with interface, implemented with implements
Variables are implicitly public static final (constants)
A class can implement multiple interfaces
Java 8+ supports default and static methods

g.data-narration: For exams: focus on interface vs abstract class differences, multiple inheritance benefits, and default/static method implications.
Quick reference: interface MyInterface {}, class MyClass implements MyInterface {}, all methods public abstract by default.
g.text: interface MyInterface { } | class MyClass implements MyInterface { }
Master interfaces to write flexible, maintainable Java code!
